twitter.hook: Nova Xavier told his mother: 'Of course it's safe, Mom. It's Wim Hof.' He'd bought Hof's $100 online course. At a community pool, his mother was timing his breath-holds when he didn't come up. He was already dead. Scott Carney documented 33 cases like this.
thread: The mechanism isn't mysterious. Voluntary hyperventilation drops blood CO₂ from ~40 mmHg to as low as 17-20 mmHg. The brain's 'breathe now' signal comes from CO₂ rising — not oxygen falling. You don't feel yourself running out of oxygen. You black out. No warning.

This is shallow-water blackout. It's well documented in the freediving literature entirely independent of Wim Hof. The WHM breathing protocol creates exactly the physiological conditions that cause it — and then practitioners go near water.

The documented cases span trained athletes, former soldiers, healthy adults, teenagers. Andrew Encinas, 27, was found 'in a meditative position' at the bottom of a backyard pool — hands clasped in front of him, no struggle, no call for help. He had lost consciousness before he could surface.
Christopher Kuyvenhoven, 23, aspiring Navy SEAL candidate. Found in a hot tub at Western Washington University after practicing WHM breathing. Submerged approximately 20 minutes before discovery.

The Metzger civil case: $67 million filed in Los Angeles Superior Court. Dismissed July 2024. The court's reason was evidentiary — causation couldn't be proven to the legal threshold. The court did NOT rule that WHM breathing cannot cause drowning.

Hof's attorney made it explicit: 'The question is not whether or not Hof's breathing exercises can lead to drowning.' The question was only whether this specific case could be proven. That's an important legal distinction most coverage missed.

The cold component adds a second layer. Mike Tipton (University of Portsmouth, The Lancet 2003): cold shock produces an immediate gasp reflex and uncontrolled hyperventilation — before you can consciously suppress it. Layer that on top of suppressed CO₂ drive and you have two independent drowning mechanisms active simultaneously.

The science behind the dry-land breathing protocol is real and stands. The PNAS 2014 study (Kox et al., 12 men, 53-57% TNF-alpha reduction) has not been retracted. Scott Carney didn't retract the science. He retracted the safety conditions around how the method is being taught.

The rule is simple: never practice WHM breathing rounds in water, near water, before swimming, or before any breath-hold in water. Practice on a mat, lying down. If you lose consciousness on a mat, nothing happens. If you lose consciousness in a pool, it kills you.
twitter.cta: The full documented file — 33 cases, the mechanism, the rules: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/practitioners/wim-hof-dangers — If you've ever practiced this near water, read it. Share it. The discourse failed people who needed this.

linkedin.hook:
Scott Carney spent a decade as Wim Hof's most prominent scientific advocate. He wrote the book. He became a certified instructor. He trained thousands of people.

By July 2025, he had documented at least 33 deaths linked to the practice.

He did not retract the science. He documented the mechanism — and why most practitioners never heard of it.
linkedin.body:
The Wim Hof Method 2014 PNAS study (Kox et al., Radboud University) remains one of the most remarkable physiological findings in modern research: twelve men, four days of training, voluntarily modulating what medical textbooks call the involuntary immune response. That result stands.

The application has killed at least 33 people.

The mechanism is documented in the diving-medicine literature entirely independently of anything Wim Hof has ever claimed. Voluntary hyperventilation drops blood CO₂ from its resting level of approximately 40 mmHg to as low as 17-20 mmHg. The brain's urge-to-breathe signal is triggered by rising CO₂, not falling oxygen. Practitioners who hyperventilate and then attempt breath-holds in water can lose consciousness without any sensation of air-hunger — no warning, no struggle, no call for help.

This is shallow-water blackout. It kills trained athletes as readily as beginners. It killed a 27-year-old entrepreneur in a backyard pool. A 23-year-old aspiring Navy SEAL. A teenager who had just purchased Hof's official online course.

Madelyn Rose Metzger's family filed a $67 million civil suit against Innerfire (Hof's company). The case was dismissed July 2024 on evidentiary grounds — the court could not prove she was performing WHM breathing at the time of death. The court did not rule that the breathing exercises cannot cause drowning.

The safety warnings now appear in Innerfire's current materials. They were not in those materials in 2015 and 2016, when four deaths were reported to Dutch newspaper Het Parool. Innerfire's response at the time: 'Pretty silly — but then you shouldn't do the exercises under water.'

The people who died were following official course materials. The rule they needed was absent from what they were shown.

The rule is not complicated: never perform WHM breathing rounds in water, near water, before swimming, or before any underwater activity. Practice on a mat. If you lose consciousness on a mat, nothing happens.

If you've shared the Wim Hof Method with someone, share this too.

Full documented file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/practitioners/wim-hof-dangers

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: 33 deaths. Same mechanism. Same pattern. No struggle. No cry for help. Found in a meditative position underwater. The discourse failed them.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: Scott Carney documented 33 WHM-linked drowning deaths by July 2025 — up from 13 in June 2023. All involved breathing exercises combined with water. None were caused by cold exposure alone.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: The mechanism: WHM breathing drops blood CO₂ from 40 mmHg to 17-20 mmHg. The urge to breathe comes from CO₂ rising, not oxygen falling. Practitioners black out underwater — no warning, no struggle, no chance to surface.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: Nova Xavier, 2023: 'Of course it's safe, Mom. It's Wim Hof.' He'd bought Hof's $100 official course. His mother was timing his breath-holds at a community pool. She noticed he hadn't come up. He was already dead.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: "The question is not whether or not Hof's breathing exercises can lead to drowning." — Hof's own attorney, on the Metzger $67M civil case dismissed July 2024

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: The PNAS 2014 science is real. The deaths are real. Both can be true. The rule that would have prevented all 33 cases: never practice WHM breathing rounds in or near water.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: Carney (2025) Substack · Tipton, The Lancet (2003) · Bart & Lau, StatPearls (2023) · Kumar & Ng, MJA (2010) · Metzger v. Innerfire, LA Superior Court

reddit.title: The 33 documented deaths: the shared mechanism and why the safety rule was absent from official materials when people needed it
reddit.body:
This is not a post saying the Wim Hof Method doesn't work. The PNAS 2014 paper is solid. The peer-reviewed physiology stands.

This is about what's killed 33 documented practitioners and why most of them never received the information that would have prevented it.

The mechanism:
VHM breathing rounds drop blood CO₂ from roughly 40 mmHg to as low as 17-20 mmHg (Bart & Lau, StatPearls 2023). The urge to breathe is triggered by rising CO₂, not falling oxygen. Practitioners who hyperventilate and then do breath-holds near water can lose consciousness without any air-hunger warning — no feeling, no struggle, no cry for help. This is shallow-water blackout. It's well documented in freediving medicine independent of WHM.

The cases follow the same pattern: no struggle, found in a meditative or relaxed position, often in a pool they entered voluntarily to 'do their WHM.' They include trained athletes, former soldiers, teenagers, adults with years of practice.

The Carney timeline: he documented 13 drownings in June 2023, updated to 21 deaths + 18 injuries by January 2024, 33 deaths by July 2025. He spent a decade as WHM's most prominent scientific advocate. He did not retract the science. He retracted the safety conditions.

The legal timeline: Metzger v. Innerfire ($67M) was dismissed July 2024. Reason: the court could not prove the girl was performing WHM breathing at the time of death. The court did NOT rule that WHM breathing cannot cause drowning. Hof's own attorney acknowledged: 'The question is not whether or not Hof's breathing exercises can lead to drowning.'

The safety warnings now exist in Innerfire's current materials. They were absent from the materials practitioners who died in 2015-2016 were following.

The rule: never practice WHM breathing rounds in water, near water, or before any underwater activity. Practice lying down on a mat.

Full breakdown with all case citations: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/practitioners/wim-hof-dangers

reddit.title: Shallow-water blackout and the Wim Hof Method: what the physiology actually explains, who's died, and the one rule that would have prevented all of it
reddit.body:
As someone who's been following the WHM evidence closely, the deaths frustrate me specifically because the mechanism is so well understood and so preventable.

Shallow-water blackout is documented in the freediving literature with no reference to Wim Hof at all. The principle: voluntary hyperventilation depletes blood CO₂ before a dive. CO₂ (not oxygen) is what triggers the urge to breathe. Hyperventilatng freediver enters water → CO₂ remains low throughout the breath-hold → O₂ falls to blackout threshold → diver loses consciousness → drowns. No warning. No struggle.

The WHM breathing protocol is physiologically identical to the freediving hyperventilation that the entire dive community has a hard safety prohibition against. The only difference is that the freediving community has effectively communicated 'never hyperventilate before water entry' and the WHM ecosystem largely hadn't — especially in earlier official materials.

Scott Carney's documented case count: 33 deaths as of July 2025. Former soldiers. Trained athletes. A 17-year-old. A 27-year-old entrepreneur found 'in a meditative position' at the bottom of a pool — hands clasped in front of his chest, no struggle. A teenager whose mother was timing his breath-holds from the pool deck.

The California civil case (Metzger v. Innerfire) was dismissed on evidentiary grounds, not on a finding that the exercises are safe. Hof's attorney's own words: 'The question is not whether or not Hof's breathing exercises can lead to drowning.' That sentence is in the public record.

The PNAS 2014 science is real. The dry-land protocol doesn't carry this risk. The rule: practice breathing rounds on a mat, never near water, never before swimming or diving.

Full breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/practitioners/wim-hof-dangers

email.subject: 33 deaths. Same mechanism. All preventable.
email.preheader: Scott Carney documented them. The physiology explains them. The rule is simple — and was absent from the official materials.
email.body:
Nova Xavier told his mother 'of course it's safe' before going to the community pool to practice his Wim Hof breath-holds. He had purchased the official $100 online course.

She was timing his holds when he didn't surface. He was already dead.

Scott Carney, who spent a decade as WHM's most prominent scientific advocate, documented at least 33 deaths like this by July 2025 — all sharing the same mechanism: voluntary hyperventilation drops blood CO₂ so efficiently that the urge-to-breathe signal disappears. Practitioners lose consciousness underwater without warning.

The PNAS 2014 science stands. The dry-land protocol doesn't carry this risk. The rule that would have prevented all 33 cases: never practice WHM breathing rounds in or near water.

Full documented file — cases, mechanism, legal record, safe protocol: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/practitioners/wim-hof-dangers

If you've shared the method with someone, share this too.

pullQuotes: 33 deaths. No struggle. No cry for help. Found in a meditative position underwater.
The urge to breathe comes from CO2 rising — not oxygen falling. You don't feel it coming.
Hof's attorney: 'The question is NOT whether breathing exercises can lead to drowning.'
The rule was absent from official materials when the people who died needed it.
Carney retracted the man. He did not retract the science.
